summary: I was born in the Nue Poe refugee camp in Thailand in 1999. My parents and sister fled Burma in 1997 because of the civil war and settled in this camp. Life there was hard; we didn’t have freedom to leave or find work, and food was scarce. Although I had a chance to go to school, educational opportunities were very limited. This difficult life in Nue Poe is why my family decided to move to the United States in 2012.

Thailand was where I was born and where my family lived in the Nue Poe refugee camp after fleeing Burma in 1997. Even though we were safe from the war, life in Thailand was restrictive; we couldn’t leave the camp or work freely, and food was scarce. I went to school there but didn’t have many chances to learn. In 2012, my family decided to leave Thailand and move to the United States for a better life. Leaving Thailand was emotional, especially saying goodbye to my cousin and friends.

My parents and sister were born in Burma, and all my relatives come from there. Because of the civil war in Burma, my family had to leave in 1997 and seek safety in Thailand’s Nue Poe refugee camp. The conflict in Burma shaped my family’s journey and the hardships we faced as refugees. Even though I was born in Thailand, my roots and family history are deeply connected to Burma. The war in Burma was the reason we eventually moved to the United States in 2012.

My family and I were resettled in the United States in 2012 after living in a refugee camp in Thailand. I was very excited when my mom told me we were moving to America because I wanted better opportunities, especially for education. Arriving in the U.S. was overwhelming since we didn’t know English and had never been on an airplane before. Now, living here, I have access to education, safety, and a happy life with good friends. The United States gave my family a fresh start after years of hardship.

When we first arrived in the United States, we landed at the New York airport. We were confused and didn’t know how to get to Minnesota, where we were resettled. We waited there for almost an hour with no guidance until a kind man helped us find our connecting flight. That moment was stressful but also a relief because we finally knew where to go next. New York was our first stop in America, marking the beginning of our new journey.

Minnesota is where my family and I finally settled after arriving in the United States in 2012. It was my first time seeing snow, and I was surprised by how cold it was. When we landed at the Minneapolis–Saint Paul International Airport, my cousin and friends were there to welcome us, which made me feel joyful and tired at the same time. Living in Minnesota has given me the chance to get a good education and live a safe, happy life. It truly became our new home after years of uncertainty.

When we arrived at the Minneapolis–Saint Paul International Airport in Minnesota, my cousin and some friends were waiting to pick us up. Seeing familiar faces after a long journey made me feel both joyful and weary. It was a warm welcome that helped me feel more at home in this new place. This airport marked the end of our long travel from Thailand and the start of our life in the United States. It was a very important moment for my family and me.
